titleEn: Were It Not For God's Power

verses.sadr: عبل إن كان ظل القسطل الحلك
وسائلي السيف عني هل ضربت به
وسائلي الرمح عني هل طعنت به

verses.ajuz: وأتبع القرن لا أخشى من الدرك

verses.sadr: كم ضربة لي بحد السيف قاطعة

translationEn: Were it not for God whose power even kings revere, I would have made the back of my steed a dome spanning the heavens. O Abla, if the darkness of battle's dust cloud hid my fighting from your eyes, then ask my horse if I ever set it free except against a troop dark as night. Ask my sword if I ever struck with it on a day of fierce combat except at the head of a king. Ask my spear if I ever thrust it except into the armored man between throat and chin. How many a clean sword-blow and spear-thrust have I delivered, shaking the saddle-bow to its base.
context: A proud fakhr poem in which Antara invites Abla to question his weapons—his horse, sword, and spear—as witnesses to his valor. The repetitive imperative 'ask' (سائلي) creates a rhetorical crescendo celebrating his martial supremacy.

titleAr: ريح الحجاز بحق من أنشاك ردي السلام
titleEn: Wind of Hijaz, Carry My Greeting

verses.sadr: يا ريح لولا أن فيك بقية
verses.ajuz: من طيب عبلة مت قبل لقاك

verses.sadr: يا عبل ما أخشى الحمام وإنما
يا عبل لا يحزنك بعدي وابشري
هلا سألت الخيل يا ابنة مالك
verses.ajuz: إن كان بعض عداك قد أغراك

translationEn: He begs the wind of Hijaz to carry his greeting to Abla, whose fragrance alone keeps him alive. He fears not death for himself but grieves at the thought of Abla weeping. From distant Syria he reassures her of his safety, recounting how he crushed the Persians so thoroughly that even angels in the heavens heard the tumult, and then magnanimously forgave his vanquished enemies.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: created you / brought you into being

context: This poem mingles tender ghazal longing with fakhr boasting, addressing the Hijaz wind as a messenger to Abla while narrating campaigns in Syria and against the Persians. It illustrates Antara's characteristic blending of love poetry with martial glory.

titleAr: وما كنت لولا حب عبلة حائلا
titleEn: Without Abla's Love I Would Not Have Stayed

verses.sadr: وما كنت لولا حب عبلة حائلا

translationEn: Without love for Abla, the poet would not have lingered in the land where the desert shrubs grow and the beloved dwells. He hopes she may see the lightning flash over the protected pastures, and gather the fruit of the arāk trees with her own hands—a tender image of pastoral intimacy and longing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a toothbrush tree / used for siwak

context: A brief lyrical fragment from Antara's ghazal tradition, invoking the landscape of the beloved's homeland as a metaphor for yearning. The desert flora—ghada shrubs and arāk trees—serve as markers of the beloved's territory.

titleEn: Long Have I Lingered at the Ruins

verses.ajuz: أسل الديار كفعل من لم يذهل

verses.sadr: لما سمعت دعاء مرة إذ دعا
إني امرؤ من خير عبس منصبا
إذ لا أبادر في المضيق فوارسي

verses.ajuz: لا بد أن أسقى بكأس المنهل
أني امرؤ سأموت إن لم أقتل

verses.sadr: وإذا حملت على الكريهة لم أقل

translationEn: The poem opens at the ruins of a beloved campsite where the poet stands bewildered, tears falling like scattered pearls at a dove's lament. He then shifts to a proud remembrance of battle: when the tribe of 'Abs called, he answered with spear and sword, routing the Banu 'Awf and standing foremost under the war-banner. He endures hunger willingly and never retreats, declaring that death is a watering-hole every man must drink from—and that if death were to take human form, it would look like him in desperate straits.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: open courtyards of a deserted camp

context: One of Antara's major qasidas combining the traditional nasib (amatory prelude at ruins) with powerful fakhr. The famous closing verse 'I shall die if not killed' became proverbial for the warrior's acceptance of fate and disdain for cowardice.

titleEn: Ubaylahs Wonder at the Warrior

verses.sadr: لا يكتسى إلا الحديد إذا اكتسى
قد طال ما لبس الحديد فإنما

verses.ajuz: لا خير فيك كأنها لم تحفل

verses.sadr: يا عبل كم من غمرة زهاءها
إما تريني قد نحلت ومن يكن
ذكر أشق به الجماجم في الوغى

translationEn: Ubayla mocks the poet for his unkempt warrior's appearance—gaunt, pale, wearing nothing but iron, with rust on his very skin—while he turns her ridicule into pride, explaining that it is this iron-clad body that has laid low fat, pampered heroes like her husband. The poem then pivots to an extended and exquisite description of his war-horse: its chest like smooth stone, its nostrils like the dens of a porcupine, its back like a stag's, its hooves hard as flint, its gait like a drinker in a hurry—on this steed he plunges into battle like a diving eagle.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: one who appears worn and uncouth

context: This major qasida showcases Antara's mastery of the horse description (waṣf al-faras), one of the longest such passages in his diwan. The dramatic reversal—woman's scorn becoming proof of the warrior's transcendent qualities—is a hallmark of his self-presentation.

titleEn: Our Spears Suffice Our Household's Need

verses.sadr: احذر محل السوء لا تحلل به

translationEn: The ostriches stride around the empty campsite in circles, like Christians processing around a temple. The poet warns against settling in a place of evil—if a dwelling site becomes hostile, move on. Our spears alone are enough to provide for our household; let him who fails to act be shamed.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: idiom: may he be shamed / disgraced

context: A brief wisdom poem combining an evocative desert scene—ostriches circling like pilgrims—with practical tribal ethics: choose your dwelling wisely and rely on your own weapons, not others' charity.

titleEn: Tears That Stream Upon the Cheeks

verses.sadr: وها أنا ميت إن لم يعني

translationEn: Tears stream constantly down his cheeks and sleep never visits his eyes. A lover tormented by separation cannot settle or forget, no matter how long the journey stretches. Even reunion failed to quench the fire within or cool the fever of desire. He asks from time only a pure life, yet time is miserly. The final verse is his cry: he will die unless patient endurance helps him bear love's sovereignty.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: a channel / stream of flowing tears

context: A moving ghazal focused entirely on the emotional devastation of separation, notable for the paradox that even reunion could not extinguish the inner fire—a mystical note on love's insatiable nature.

titleEn: They Relieved My Anguish and Healed My Ailment

verses.sadr: قسما يا عبل يا أخت المها

translationEn: The poet calls on his enemies to bring out their champions so he may grant them bitter drafts from his sword's edge, and taunts the Persians for their hesitation. Then in a sudden turn of tenderness, he swears by Abla's sweet smile and her kohl-lined eyes—the very visitant dream of her is the only sleep he knows. He closes by blessing the nights past in her company with a prayer for abundant rain.
context: This poem dramatically contrasts martial bravado—taunting the Persians in single combat—with intimate sworn devotion to Abla. The oath by her smile and her eyes is among the most tender passages in Antara's diwan.

titleEn: When the East Wind Blows at Dusk

verses.sadr: ألا يا عبل إن خانوا عهودي

translationEn: When the east wind blows at dusk, it heals his sick heart—but it brings news that his tribe has departed with his beloved, leaving him prostrated in the sand valley. He endured their broken pledges and her father's ingratitude, bearing humiliation against his will. The separation-crow has persecuted him as if he had slain one of its kin. A bird's lament on the branches mirrors his own weeping; he lends it his eyelids to cry with. He confesses that illness has become so familiar that he feels sick when well—and beneath his armor lies only a wasted silhouette whose inner blade of will still defies the sharpest sword.
context: A layered elegy of separation where nature—wind, bird, ruins—is enlisted as co-sufferer. The final image of a 'soul-blade' inside the wasted body that even a polished sword cannot dull is a metaphor for indomitable spirit beneath physical decay.

titleEn: I Fight Every Tyrant Yet Separation Kills Me

verses.sadr: غراب البين ما لك كل يوم

translationEn: He stands at crumbling ruins in the sandy valley, erased by the north wind, weeping over traces of the beloved's tribe. The separation-crow torments him daily; he addresses it half-jokingly, half-desperately, asking it to heal his heart and bring news of Ubayla. His heart wanders every land following camel tracks while his body lies as a ghost hoping for the ghost of a dream. He outweeps even the lamenting bird, and closes with the poem's immortal paradox: I fight every tyrant in battle, yet separation kills me without a single blow.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: ruins of a camp / traces of habitation

context: This poem contains one of the most celebrated lines in pre-Islamic Arabic poetry: the paradox of a warrior who defeats all enemies yet is slain by separation. It exemplifies the fusion of fakhr and ghazal that defines Antara's unique poetic voice.

titleEn: Perhaps the Days Will Bring Relief Soon
translationEn: He addresses the noblewoman's daughter with masochistic devotion: her torment is sweet and her father's injustice a form of fairness, because they belong to a clan whose glory places them above the stars. His tribe's racism—mocking his dark-skinned mother—brings him no shame; in every battle he has tales that make heroes cower. He shackles the necks of enemies, protects women with his sword, and willingly endures humiliation for those he loves. He closes with a fragile hope: perhaps after this bitter estrangement life will sweeten again.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the iron collar / chains of captivity

context: A psychologically complex poem where Antara simultaneously submits to the beloved's family hierarchy and asserts absolute martial supremacy—the paradox of a warrior-slave who is master of the battlefield yet servant in love.

titleEn: The Generous Feeders in Years of Drought

verses.sadr: فلئن صرمت الحبل يا ابنة مالك
من مثل قومي حين يختلف القنا
إنا إذا حمس الوغى نروي القنا

translationEn: A grand tribal panegyric (fakhr) beginning at eroded ruins where wind and rain have erased the beloved's traces. Antara demands that the daughter of Malik ask the kings and tribes for his record in battle: he is death incarnate when spears clash, leaving slain warriors for the wolves of the wilderness. He catalogues specific enemies routed—the tribes of Dabba, Banu Sabah—and extols his clan of 'Abs as the generous feeders when drought stretches across the land, the saviors in crisis, the protectors of women on the day of reckoning, and those who rein in the arrogant. Their swords reignite wars that have gone cold, and in times of catastrophe they are the refuge of the perishing.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the cry for help / the distressed
female ghouls / demons (simile for lean horses)

context: One of the longest fakhr poems in the batch, celebrating the 'Abs tribe's martial prowess, generosity in drought, and role as tribal protectors. The closing refrain 'the generous feeders when the years pile up in drought' became a celebrated image of Arab tribal virtue.

titleAr: لا يشرب الخمر إلا من له ذمم
titleEn: Only the Honorable May Drink Wine

verses.sadr: لا يشرب الخمر إلا من له ذمم
verses.ajuz: ولا يبيت له جار على وجل

verses.sadr: ولا تقتض الدين إلا بالقنا الذبل
verses.ajuz: ولا تحكم سوى الأسياف في القلل

verses.sadr: ولا تفر إذا ما خضت معركة
verses.ajuz: فما يزيد فرار المرء في الأجل

verses.sadr: يا عبل أنت سواد القلب فاحتكمي
verses.ajuz: في مهجتي واعدلي يا غاية الأمل

verses.sadr: وإن ترحلت من عبس فلا تقفي
verses.ajuz: في دار ذل ولا تصغي إلى العذل
تبقى بلا فارس يدعى ولا بطل

verses.sadr: سلي فزارة عن فعلي وقد نفرت
تهز سمر القنا حقدا علي وقد
يخبرك بدر بن عمرو أنني بطل
verses.ajuz: ألقى الجيوش بقلب قد من جبل
والطعن في إثرهم أمضى من الأجل
وما أبكي لفرقة أصحاب ولا طلل

verses.sadr: بل من فراق التي في جفنها سقم
verses.ajuz: قد زادني عللا منه على عللي

verses.sadr: أمسي على وجل خوف الفراق كما
verses.ajuz: تمسي الأعادي من سيفي على وجل

verses.sadr: من لي برد الصبا واللهو والغزل
verses.ajuz: هيهات ما فات من أيامك الأول

verses.sadr: طوى الجديدان ما قد كنت أنشره
وما ثنى الدهر عزمي عن مهاجمة
في الخيل والخافقات السود لي شغل

verses.ajuz: فلست أبكي على رسم ولا طلل
هل فاتني بطل أو حلت عن بطل

verses.sadr: لا يشرب الخمر إلا من له ذمم
verses.ajuz: ولا يبيت له جار على وجل

translationEn: An aristocratic code of honor opens and closes this poem: only the man of pledges may drink wine, only the man whose neighbor sleeps safely at night. Debts are collected only with spears, judgments rendered only by swords. He counsels Abla to leave any abode of humiliation and ignore blamers, for without him the tribe's land would be empty of heroes. He challenges the tribe of Fazara, whose cavalry Badr ibn 'Amr can attest he shattered, returning from battle so joyful he was like a drunken celebrant. Yet separation frightens him as much as his sword frightens his enemies—and the poet laments the lost days of youth and love, now folded away by time.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the dark of the heart (deepest part / core)
the din and heat of battle

context: A poem of moral instruction framed around the warrior's code, containing the celebrated opening aphorism that wine belongs only to the man of honor. It moves through martial boasting to tender regret for lost youth and love.

titleEn: Life with Humiliation Is Hell

verses.ajuz: واقدم إذا حق اللقا في الأول
أو مت كريما تحت ظل القسطل

verses.sadr: موت الفتى في عزه خير له
verses.ajuz: من أن يبيت أسير طرف أكحل

verses.sadr: إن كنت في عدد العبيد فهمتي
قد طال عزكم وذلي في الهوى

translationEn: One of Antara's most aphoristic poems, opening with wisdom maxims of warrior ethics: face oppression with equal force, ignore cowards' counsel, choose an elevated station or die nobly in battle—for no fortress saves you from fate. He declares that though counted among slaves, his ambition soars above the Pleiades, and that his spear and sword—not kinship or numbers—earned him his glory. He enumerates enemies slain from Banu Huraiqa to Rabi'a and Zubarqan, then in a stunning reversal describes his dark-skinned mother with affection: legs like an ostrich's, hair like peppercorns, and a smile beneath the veil like lightning in deep darkness. He closes refusing the water of life drunk in humiliation, preferring the bitter cup of colocynth drunk in honor.
context: This poem contains Antara's most famous statement of identity pride: his aspiration soars above the stars even if he is counted among slaves. The tender description of his African mother, ending with her smile like lightning, is remarkable for its time in humanizing an enslaved woman.

titleEn: A Heart the Blamer Cannot Sway
translationEn: His heart cannot be swayed by reproach and his eyes barely sleep—misfortune has hardened him until fate's beauty and ugliness feel alike. He turns to address a rival named 'Amr who has threatened him with empty words: we shall see which of us is left pierced on the ground, whose wife is taken captive and weeps in bereavement. You dare remember Abla while she is guarded by a fearsome lion (Antara himself), and you seek to face me whose sword the very mountain could not withstand.
context: A tight, aggressive poem of challenge directed at a named enemy 'Amr, combining the lover's sleepless longing with the warrior's direct confrontation. The image of a lion guarding Abla's tent identifies Antara himself as the guardian beast.

titleEn: Come Fight Me, O Misfortunes of Nights

verses.sadr: إن لي همة أشد من الصخر
وجوادا ما سار إلا سرى البرق
يا سباع الفلا إذا اشتعل الحرب
ثم عودي من بعد ذا واشكريني

translationEn: Antara defies the misfortunes of the night to attack him from right and left—he barely even notices them. His resolve is harder than rock, stronger than fixed mountains. His spear-point guides him through darkness, and his black horse with a crescent blaze between its eyes runs like lightning whose hooves spark fire; he and the horse ransom each other with their lives. When the marketplace of battle opens and burnished blades blaze, he is its broker and his lance the merchant buying precious souls. He then turns to invite the beasts of the wilderness to follow him to the feast of enemies' skulls, which he offers as food for their cubs.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the white blaze on a horse's forehead

context: A defiant and richly imagined fakhr poem notable for its invitation to the beasts of prey to follow Antara into battle as scavengers—a dark inversion of pastoral imagery that underscores his absolute self-confidence in battle.

titleAr: سلي يا عبل عمرا عن فعالي
titleEn: Ask Amr About My Deeds, O Abla

verses.sadr: سلي يا عبل عمرا عن فعالي

verses.ajuz: إذا ما قال ظنك في مقالي

verses.sadr: إذا ما سل سال دما نجيعا

translationEn: He asks Abla to consult 'Amr about how he answered the enemies who attacked in darkness on lean-flanked horses, each a tyrant twisting his mustache. When they kindled the fire of death with their spear-tips, a black lion of 'Abs quenched it with a polished bright sword that, when drawn, poured dark blood and would pierce even solid mountains. His spear-tip glows like a crescent moon, and when he wields it in his right hand, death races to keep up on his left. He kept his promise to Abla, for had he broken it, the base men would have said he grew cold toward her—an unacceptable dishonor.
context: A compact fakhr poem structured as a report to Abla about a night raid, framed by the motive of keeping his pledge to her. The closing verse ties martial honor directly to love's fidelity—breaking his promise would prove him faithless to her.

titleEn: He Desires the One Who Torments Him

verses.sadr: لو كان قلبي معي ما اخترت غيركم
verses.ajuz: ولا رضيت سواكم في الهوى بدلا
فليس يقبل لا لوما ولا عذلا

translationEn: If his heart were his own, he would choose no one but the beloved and accept no substitute in love. But his heart is a willful runaway that desires precisely the one who torments it, and so refuses to accept any blame or rebuke—for the heart is beyond reason when love commands it.
context: A brief philosophical ghazal couplet on the paradox of a heart that chooses its own tormentor—a theme that anticipates later Sufi poetry about the soul that loves what destroys it.

titleAr: دع ما مضى لك في الزمان الأول
titleEn: Leave What Has Passed in Former Times

verses.sadr: دع ما مضى لك في الزمان الأول
إن كنت أنت قطعت برا مقفرا

verses.ajuz: لا مؤنس لي غير حد المنصل

translationEn: A poem of self-comparison and boast: he tells a rival to leave past glories aside and act in the present. If the rival crossed empty deserts at night with an army, Antara traveled alone under the Pleiades, companioned only by his sword-edge. He describes a supernatural night journey: the full moon racing overhead like a hurrying rider, the vulture hurtling toward the Arcturus star, a ghoul flickering before him like a torch with red eyes and scythe-like claws, while jinn howl in the forests—and all of them scatter screaming at the sight of his sword, like camels panicking around a camp. These are nights whose mere retelling would turn a newborn's hair white.
context: A vivid supernatural night journey narrative that reads like a folkloric adventure, situating Antara's heroism in a cosmos of stars, ghouls, and jinn—all of whom flee his sword. This nightscape poetry is rare in pre-Islamic verse and reflects the genre's imaginal possibilities.

titleEn: The Punishment of Estrangement Brought Union
translationEn: The patient endurance of estrangement ultimately brings reunion, and true patience reveals what seemed impossible. He freed noble women and maidens, leaving no one in bondage—driven only by love for Abla. He blames fate for humbling a man of his resolve. The body of the poem records a battle against the combined tribes of Tayy and Kalb who arrived with an earth-filling army on lean war-horses; they fled in confusion, while only a slave—Antara himself—turned back the reins of war when the noblemen of lineage faltered. The enemy cavalry fled lighter than they came, their fallen chiefs used as horseshoes by their own panicked mounts.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the impossible / what was deemed unattainable
the women's riding litters / the women's camp

context: A remarkable poem where Antara explicitly names himself 'a slave' who alone turned back the enemy when noblemen of pedigree failed—a pointed assertion that courage, not birth, determines nobility. The image of the enemy's skulls becoming horseshoes for their own fleeing cavalry is among his most grotesque battle images.

titleAr: يا صاحبي لا تبك ربعا قد خلا
titleEn: Friend, Do Not Weep at the Empty Campsite

verses.sadr: يا صاحبي لا تبك ربعا قد خلا
من أين تدري الدار أنك عاشق

verses.ajuz: لو لم يذق مني المرارة ماحلا
دارت بها في الغاب غربان الفلا
إن كنتما عن أرض عبس تعدلا
خط المشيب على شبابي ما علا

verses.sadr: بل لو صدمت بهمتي جبلي حرى
لو لم تكن يا قيس غرك جاهل
verses.ajuz: ما سقت نحو ديار عنتر جحفلا

verses.sadr: يا قيس أنت تعد نفسك سيدا

verses.ajuz: إن كنت ممن عقله قد أكملا

translationEn: He urges his companion to stop weeping at empty ruins—the house does not know your love, and the only true messenger when a friend betrays is the spear-point. He has hardened fate itself to bitterness. He then commissions his two companions as messengers to 'Abs: tell Qays and al-Rabi' that grey hair cannot dim my resolve—if I struck the mountains Hira and Abu Qubays with my will, they would shake. He warns Qays that it was a fool's counsel that led him against Antara's territory, and advises him to follow his noble father's example instead of seeking blood feuds with Fazara. The closing line: I left nothing in their homeland except keening women wailing in the open desert.
context: A poem of combined wisdom and warning, notable for its dramatic embassy structure: two companions carry his message to named tribal enemies. The closing image of empty land filled only with wailing women is his signature battle's-aftermath motif.

titleEn: Have the Poets Left Any Theme Untouched

verses.ajuz: أم هل عرفت الدار بعد توهم

verses.sadr: ولقد شربت من المدامة بعد ما
وإذا صحوت فما أقصر عن ندى
هلا سألت الخيل يا ابنة مالك
verses.ajuz: إن كنت جاهلة بما لم تعلمي

verses.sadr: إذ لا أزال على رحالة سابح

verses.ajuz: مني وبيض الهند تقطر من دمي

verses.sadr: يا شاة ما قنص لمن حلت له

verses.ajuz: إذ تقلص الشفتان عن وضح الفم

verses.sadr: في حومة الحرب التي لا تشتكي
إذ يتقون بي الأسنة لم أخم

verses.ajuz: ما قد علمت وبعض ما لم تعلمي
وزوت جواني الحرب من لم يجرم

verses.sadr: ولقد خشيت بأن أموت ولم تدر

translationEn: This is the opening of Antara's immortal Mu'allaqa—one of the seven great odes of pre-Islamic Arabia. It begins with the famous rhetorical question 'Have the poets left anything to say?' at the deserted campsite of the beloved. He describes her with intoxicating similes: eyes of a solitary gazelle fawn, breath like a perfume merchant's sachet, or a virgin meadow watered by untainted rains. She sleeps on soft cushions while he sleeps on the saddle-back of his black bridled horse. A long camel description follows her. He boasts that he is gentle when treated justly and terrible when wronged. He describes killing a warrior whose armor he tears open, leaving him for the vultures—and the moment of inspiration in battle when the cavalry cry 'Advance, Antara!' heals his soul. The poem ends with the enmity of the sons of Dumdum who vowed his blood, but whom he answers: their father I already left as carrion for eagles.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the theme that has been exhausted / trodden over
the back teeth / grinning with bared teeth

context: This is the Mu'allaqa of Antara ibn Shaddad, the most celebrated poem in his diwan and one of the seven canonical Mu'allaqat of pre-Islamic Arabia. Its opening question and its description of the beloved have been quoted and analyzed more than any other passage in his work. The poem integrates nasib, fakhr, battle narrative, and self-defense in a single unified masterpiece.

titleEn: With Every Keen Blade That Pierces Through
translationEn: He praises his warrior companions armed with keen blades that pierce between the ribs like the collar of a leather jerkin. His horsemen march with coats of fine Syrian mail upon them, glowing like burning coals. Among them are free, bright-faced men of trust, unlike those dark-faced treacherous tribes he has seen. When their camels broke into a run toward the enemy's watering pools, they chose between killing and capturing spoils. And if an ally betrays, they are leopards who mark noses—a vivid image of unmistakable vengeance.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: penetration / the ability to pierce through
we mark their noses / we take vengeance visibly

context: A tightly crafted fakhr fragment celebrating Antara's warrior band and their code of combat and loyalty. The simile of men blazing like burning coals in their mail is one of his characteristic light-and-fire images in battle description.

titleEn: Ruqash Comes Only for Brief Visits

verses.sadr: عجوز من بني حام بن نوح:

translationEn: A poem that begins with a resigned lover's lament—Ruqash visits only rarely and the bond with her is like a worn-out rope. He stands with companions at a watering stop, spotting women's litters moving in the dusk. Then the poem pivots to a thrilling battle scene: his trotting war-horses scatter dust and death, facing an army of blood-thirsty warriors armed with Indian blades like flames. They come like a cold cloud-front; Antara's force arrives as wildfire in a thicket. He silences all sounds but the din of combat, scattering their ranks on his battle-scarred horse whose saddle-straps are like fine cloth ribbons. The poem closes with self-identification: led by a young man of the finest 'Abs stock, whose mother is of the line of Ham son of Noah—the poet's proud claim of his African maternal heritage, with his mother's forehead compared to the sacred black stone.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: women's litters / travelling caravan of women
a thin fine curtain / decorated cloth
the sacred stone of the Standing Place (at Mecca)

context: This poem's remarkable closing image—comparing his mother's forehead to the sacred Maqam stone at Mecca—is both a boast of her dignity and a spiritual elevation of his African lineage. The poem moves from melancholic ghazal to furious battle narrative to proud tribal self-definition.

titleEn: Abla Lingers in Her Veil While I Fight

verses.sadr: لما سمعت نداء مرة قد علا

translationEn: Abla rests in her veiled seclusion trailing her robes while Antara is encased in the links of dark iron. He sees spoils he could seize but his sense of modesty and propriety holds him back. He wishes she could see him advancing in battle like a lion. The enemies are small as locusts and large as frogs in a flooded pond. When the cry of Murra and the sons of Rabi'a rose in the dark dust, and Muhallem led his men under a banner of death, he knew there would be a blow that scatters nestlings from their perches. They call his name while swords flash like lightning in a dark cloud and armor gleams like frogs' eyes in a dark pool—and their wives bring medicinal herbs to heal the fallen.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the iron chain-links / coat of mail

context: A poem rich in zoological similes for the enemy—frogs, locusts, nestlings—contrasted with Antara as a lion. The detail that his restraint (tashum) prevents him from taking spoils is a rare moment of code-ethics in his battle poetry, underlining his self-control even at victory.

titleEn: I Wept at Bitter Separation Though Steadfast

verses.ajuz: صبور على طعن القنا لو علمتم

verses.sadr: وأطمع من دهري بما لا أناله
verses.ajuz: وألزم منه ذل من ليس يرحم

verses.sadr: وأرجو التداني منك يا ابنة مالك

verses.ajuz: إذا عاد عني كيف بات المتيم

verses.sadr: ولا تجزعي إن لج قومك في دمي
verses.ajuz: فما لي بعد الهجر لحم ولا دم

verses.sadr: ألم تسمعي نوح الحمائم في الدجى
ولم يبق لي يا عبل شخص معرف
وإن عشت من بعد الفراق فما أنا
وإن نام جفني كان نومي علالة

translationEn: He wept at the agony of separation yet is steadfast against lance-thrusts—a paradox he offers to those who do not know him. He will conceal his longing in his heart, stay awake while blamers sleep, and hope for approach to Abla though fire-wars stand between. He begs her to send her phantom image in a dream and ask it how the infatuated one fared. He tells her not to grieve if her people seek his blood—there is no flesh or blood left in him after estrangement. Doves in the darkness learned their mourning from his grief. All that remains of him is a burning liver dissolving into illness, decaying bones, and a ribcage besieged by the army of rejection. If he survives separation, his love-claim will ring hollow—and his sleep is nothing but a hoping for the visitant dream.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the burning / scorched liver (center of emotion)

context: A deeply introspective ghazal focusing on the progressive physical dissolution of the lover's body under separation's siege. The image of the dove learning its mourning from the poet's grief is a reversal of the usual nasib convention where the bird provokes human tears.

titleEn: That Is Abla's Fire, O My Companion
translationEn: He spots Abla's campfire in the pitch-dark night and tells his drinking companion—that fire has lit up the darkness, and its twin burns in his own heart as a fire of longing that only grows when fanned. A white-skinned maiden swaying like a branch in the breeze kindled it; her saliva is sweeter than honey mixed with wine, yet every cool sip from her lips burns like hellfire in his mouth. The full moon stole its beauty from her, and gazelles of the wilderness borrowed their magic from her eyelids. His passion is everlasting torment. He then pivots to praise a patron king: kings prostrate themselves at his mere mention, and when he marches, death races ahead of him to reach his enemies before he arrives.
context: A poem that opens with a campfire becoming a symbol of dual fire—the beloved's hearth and the lover's heart. The paradox of the beloved's cool lips tasting like hellfire is a sophisticated oxymoron. The closing panegyric to a powerful unnamed king suggests court performance context.

titleEn: One Day in Honor's Shade Surpasses a Thousand
translationEn: Zubaiba reproaches him for his recklessness in the crush of battle, fearing he will meet death by spear or sword. He rejects her counsel as unworthy of noble men—only the base accept such logic. A gray-bearded elder plunges into the sea of death and returns unharmed, while death comes for an infant in its cradle before weaning. His maxim: do not accept diminishment and humiliation, settling for scraps. One day lived in honor's shadow is worth more than a thousand years in the shade of abasement.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the crumbled / broken remnants of worldly goods

context: One of Antara's most frequently quoted wisdom poems, built around the immortal closing aphorism contrasting one day of honor against a millennium of humiliation—a verse that became proverbial in Arabic literature and is still widely cited.

titleAr: أحب بني عبس ولو هدروا دمي
titleEn: I Love the Banu Abs Even If They Shed My Blood

verses.sadr: أحب بني عبس ولو هدروا دمي
سلي يا ابنة العبسي رمحي وصارمي
verses.ajuz: وما فعلا في يوم حرب الأعاجم

verses.sadr: وكم فارس يا عبل غادرت ثاويا

translationEn: He opens with the most paradoxical declaration of tribal loyalty: he loves the Banu 'Abs even if they declare his blood lawful and call him an oppressor. He then asks Abla—daughter of 'Abs—to question his spear and sword about what they did in the Persian wars. He fed them the blood of enemies mixed with bitter gall. On a pure-blooded Arab mare that flies when battle intensifies and whinnies in fear while slithering from spear-tips like a black snake, he plunged into the sea of death. Many a warrior he left behind biting his hands in remorse, while desert beasts roll his body and flocks of great vultures dive on him from the sky.

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: declared my blood permissible / halal to shed
black snakes (simile for the slithering mare)

context: The opening paradox—loving a tribe that outlaws your blood—is one of Antara's most psychologically rich statements, reflecting his ambiguous position as the illegitimate son whose valor constantly exceeds but can never fully resolve his social outsider status.

titleEn: A Heart That Wine Cannot Console

verses.sadr: ألا يا عبل قد شمت الأعادي

verses.ajuz: تشيب من له في المهد عام

translationEn: A heart that even wine cannot console, a body perpetually sick, eyelids that bleed in the night's darkness—this is the lover's state. A cooing dove wounds his heart with a sweet voice; he turns entirely to the memory of Abla, telling his companion 'this is my only aspiration.' In the Hijaz, her people have made union with her forbidden. She dwells among her tribe's tents—a full-figured, unveiled beauty whose healthy eyes conceal sickness, and whose lips hold musk and camphor mingled with wine. The full moon borrows its completeness from her face; the willow branch borrows its grace from her sway. Though enemies gloat at his distance, his journey has brought him suffering enough to whiten a newborn's hair—and then ease, and an indescribable king whose generosity flows from his palms like sea or rain-cloud. The sun has crowned this ruler; all the world's kings pray toward him; he is humanity's soul. The poem closes with a prayer: 'Advance, O master of both worlds, and endure as long as the dove laments.'

vocabularyHighlights.meaning: the crying bird / one who calls out
the stars of the Great Bear (Ursa Major)
mankind and jinn / the two weighty creations

context: The final poem of this batch blends personal ghazal anguish with a cosmic panegyric to a great ruler whose throne rests on the stars of Ursa Major and whose canopy is the sky itself. The prayer closing—'endure as long as the dove laments'—ties the love motif of mourning doves to the poem's political benediction.
